policy_statement: By 2030, transition the district system steam loop serving downtown and Civic Center to renewable energy (ES.5-5).
mechanism_description: Transitioning the district steam loop to renewable energy reduces fossil fuel use for district heat, lowering associated greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Energy supply decarbonization (district heat) → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: District energy plant(s) and steam loop operations shift from fossil-fueled boilers/CHP and fossil-derived steam to renewable/zero-carbon heat supply (e.g., large heat pumps, geothermal, solar thermal, renewable electricity, waste heat recovery, renewable fuels).  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of coal/oil/gas (and/or reduced upstream fossil fuel use) lowers direct stack CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O emissions per unit of delivered thermal energy; emissions intensity of delivered steam declines.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle CO₂e associated with district heating service for connected buildings/customers.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from replacing fossil fuel heat generation in a district energy system with renewable/zero-carbon heat.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s core causal chain is fuel/source switching in district heat supply that lowers fossil combustion and therefore reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can also improve air quality, energy efficiency, or resilience, the largest climate-relevant effect common to all such policies is direct emissions mitigation from decarbonizing heat supply.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “renewable” were implemented via high-emitting bioenergy or if upstream emissions were significant, the net benefit could vary, but the mechanism intent and typical implementation still targets emissions reduction via reduced fossil use; adaptation is not the primary pathway.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that transitions a district steam/heating loop to renewable/zero-carbon energy primarily acts by lowering the emissions intensity of delivered heat, i.e., mitigation.  
5) Note on secondary subtype: Any secondary classification (e.g., co-benefit air quality, energy efficiency gains, resilience via diversified supply) depends on policy-specific details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: “Renewable” heat could include biomass/biogas with variable lifecycle emissions; electrified district heat only delivers mitigation if the electricity supply is low-carbon. Some projects may also increase resilience (redundancy, islanding), but that is not the dominant climate pathway for this mechanism.
co_benefits: Potential air quality improvements (reduced NOx/PM), energy cost stability, improved local public health.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via infrastructure investment (e.g., heat pumps/geothermal/plant retrofit), but the text itself is framed as a goal/target without specifying funding, projects, or mandates—so classified as target-setting.
